attrs.alt: Risky Sign-Ins evaluation and response flow showing real-time and offline detection paths and sign-in risk states

content.text: Sign-In Risk Overview

A risky sign-in is any authentication event where Microsoft Entra ID Protection detects suspicious characteristics. Risk is evaluated both in real time during the sign-in and in a subsequent offline pass hours later. The resulting risk level (High, Medium, Low) is then compared against CA policy conditions that may block the sign-in or require additional authentication.
Risk Level
Meaning
Typical Source
Default CA Response
High
Strong evidence of compromise or active attack
AiTM phishing, password spray, malware IP, leaked creds used for sign-in
Block access
Medium
Suspicious but not confirmed compromise
Atypical travel, unfamiliar properties, anonymous IP
Require MFA challenge
Low
Mild anomaly; low probability of compromise
Unusual IP, minor behavioral deviation
Allow or require MFA
None
No risk signal detected
Normal sign-in from known device and location
Allow without friction

Sign-in risk and user risk feed into separate CA conditions. A user with zero sign-in risk history can still carry High user risk from leaked credentials. Deploy both sign-in risk policies and user risk policies to cover both attack vectors.
Real-Time vs Offline Detections

Detections that can be computed from the sign-in event alone (anonymous IP, known malware IP) are available in real time and can block the sign-in before the token is issued. Detections that require correlation across multiple events or across users (atypical travel, password spray) are computed offline and update the risk level hours later.
Detection Type
Timing
Why Offline?
Atypical travel
2-4 hours after sign-in
Needs second sign-in from distant location to compare
Leaked credentials
Minutes to hours
Depends on Microsoft's breach data ingestion pipeline
Password spray
1-6 hours
Requires cross-account correlation across the tenant
Suspicious inbox rules
30-60 minutes
Requires Exchange audit log ingestion
Mass access to sensitive files
1-4 hours
Requires SharePoint audit log aggregation
AiTM phishing
Real-time + offline update
Initial detection real-time; IP mismatch correlation is offline
Reading Risky Sign-Ins

The riskySignIns endpoint and the SigninLogs table both expose sign-in risk data. The Graph API gives structured per-event access with risk state filtering; Log Analytics provides historical aggregation and cross-field correlation for security investigations.

# Read risky sign-ins from the Graph API
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'IdentityRiskEvent.Read.All'

# Get all active (atRisk) sign-in risk events
$riskySignIns = Get-MgRiskySignIn -Filter "riskState eq 'atRisk'" -Top 50
foreach ($s in $riskySignIns) {
    [PSCustomObject]@{
        Id          = $s.Id
        User        = $s.UserPrincipalName
        RiskLevel   = $s.RiskLevel
        RiskState   = $s.RiskState
        RiskDetail  = $s.RiskDetail
        IPAddress   = $s.IpAddress
        Location    = $s.Location.City
        App         = $s.AppDisplayName
        CreatedAt   = $s.CreatedDateTime
    }
} | Format-Table -AutoSize

# Filter to high-risk sign-ins with AiTM detection type
$aitm = Get-MgRiskDetection -Filter \
    "riskEventType eq 'investigationsThreatIntelligence' and riskLevel eq 'high'"
CA Policies for Sign-In Risk

The recommended deployment is a three-tier policy set: block High, require MFA for Medium, and leave Low risk as a pass-through. Each tier is a separate CA policy scoped to specific risk levels. When a user satisfies the MFA grant control for a medium-risk sign-in, Identity Protection automatically remediates the sign-in risk event.
Testing with Report-Only Mode

Always deploy risk-based CA policies in Report-Only mode first. This records what would have happened — which users would have been blocked — without actually enforcing the grant control. Review the impact in SigninLogs before switching to 'enabled'.
Confirming and Dismissing Sign-In Risk Events

Admins can take two actions on risky sign-in events at the user level: confirm compromised (adds evidence of compromise to the user's risk score, prompting a forced sign-out) or dismiss (marks the event as a false positive). Both actions operate at the user level, not the individual sign-in event level.

# Confirm a risky sign-in as compromised (adds to the user's risk score)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyUsers/confirmCompromised' \
    -Body (@{ userIds = @('<user-object-id>') } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'

# Dismiss a risky sign-in event (mark as confirmed safe / false positive)
# Use the sign-in risk event ID, not the user ID
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyUsers/dismiss' \
    -Body (@{ userIds = @('<user-object-id>') } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'

# Note: dismissing the user-level risk also closes all associated sign-in risk events.
# There is no API to dismiss individual sign-in events independently of the user.

# After MFA is satisfied on a medium-risk sign-in, the risk is auto-remediated.
# Check the state via:
$event = Get-MgRiskySignIn -RiskySignInId '<signin-id>'
Write-Host "State: $($event.RiskState)"  # should be 'remediated'
Monitoring with KQL

Sign-in risk data in SigninLogs supports investigation of unblocked high-risk events (possible CA misconfiguration), remediation rates, and detection patterns. These queries should be part of a regular security review cadence.

# Query SigninLogs for all risky sign-ins in the last 7 days
// KQL — copy into Log Analytics or Sentinel
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where RiskLevelDuringSignIn in ('high', 'medium')
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, RiskLevelDuringSignIn, RiskState,
          IPAddress, Location, AppDisplayName, ConditionalAccessStatus
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// High-risk sign-ins that were NOT blocked by CA (misconfiguration signal)
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where RiskLevelDuringSignIn == 'high'
| where ResultType == 0  // succeeded
| where ConditionalAccessStatus != 'failure'  // CA didn't block it
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, AppDisplayName, ConditionalAccessPolicies

// Sign-in risk remediated by MFA (user completed MFA challenge successfully)
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where RiskLevelDuringSignIn in ('medium', 'high')
| where RiskState == 'remediated'
| summarize Count = count() by UserPrincipalName, RiskLevelDuringSignIn
| order by Count desc
AiTM Phishing — Deep Dive

Adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing is the highest-impact sign-in risk detection. The attacker deploys a reverse proxy (e.g., Evilginx2, Modlishka) that sits between the user and Entra ID. The user authenticates and completes MFA through the proxy — the attacker captures the resulting session token and replays it from a different IP address. Traditional MFA is bypassed because the user did complete it; the attacker simply stole the post-MFA token.

# Detecting AiTM sessions via Sign-in Logs correlation
# AiTM: attacker's IP appears in the sign-in; different IP used to replay the token

// KQL: find sign-ins where the authentication IP differs from the session IP
// (classic AiTM signal — user authenticates through proxy, attacker uses different IP)
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where RiskLevelDuringSignIn == 'high'
| where RiskEventTypes_V2 contains 'investigationsThreatIntelligence'
     or RiskEventTypes_V2 contains 'genericForwardingMicrosoftSignout'
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, Location,
          RiskLevelDuringSignIn, RiskState, AppDisplayName
| join kind=leftouter (
    OfficeActivity
    | where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
    | where Operation in ('FileDownloaded', 'FileCopied', 'Send')
    | project UserId, ClientIP, OfficeOperation=Operation, OfficeTime=TimeGenerated
  ) on $left.UserPrincipalName == $right.UserId
| where isnotempty(OfficeOperation)  // check for post-auth activity from different IP
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// Alternative: flag sessions using Evilginx2 / Modlishka proxy signatures
// These are included in Microsoft's threat intelligence detection set
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(24h)
| where RiskEventTypes_V2 has 'aiTMPhishing'
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, RiskLevelDuringSignIn, ConditionalAccessStatus

The only technical defense against AiTM that works at the protocol level is phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2, WHfB). These methods bind the authentication to the origin URL using cryptography — a proxy cannot forward the challenge because the response would be for the wrong origin. Require phishing-resistant MFA for all admin accounts as the first priority.
Password Spray Detection and Response

Password spray attacks try one common password against a large number of accounts to avoid per-account lockout thresholds. Identity Protection detects this cross-account pattern and elevates user risk for affected accounts. KQL queries in SigninLogs can independently detect spray patterns from failure codes before the offline detection fires.

# Password spray detection — multiple accounts tried with same password
# Identity Protection flags this as a high user risk detection at the account level

// KQL: detect password spray pattern in sign-in failures
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(1h)
| where ResultType in ('50126', '50053', '50055', '50056')
    // 50126 = invalid credentials, 50053 = account locked,
    // 50055 = expired password, 50056 = user doesn't exist
| summarize FailedAccounts = dcount(UserPrincipalName), FailedCount = count()
    by IPAddress, bin(TimeGenerated, 10m)
| where FailedAccounts > 20  // same IP, many different accounts, short window
| order by FailedCount desc

// Extended spray detection across 24h window
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(24h)
| where ResultType == '50126'
| summarize Accounts = dcount(UserPrincipalName), Attempts = count()
    by IPAddress
| where Accounts > 50
| order by Accounts desc
Streaming to Microsoft Sentinel

Sign-in risk data becomes most actionable when it is combined with other Microsoft 365 signals in Sentinel. Enable diagnostic settings to stream SigninLogs, RiskyUsers, and UserRiskEvents to a Log Analytics workspace connected to Sentinel. This enables correlation rules and SOAR playbooks that can respond to risk events within seconds.

# Configure diagnostic settings to stream Identity Protection data to Sentinel
# This is done via Azure Monitor diagnostic settings on the Entra ID tenant

# PowerShell via Azure Resource Manager REST API:
# (Equivalent to: Entra ID > Monitoring > Diagnostic Settings > + Add)
$diagBody = @{
    properties = @{
        workspaceId = '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/<ws>'
        logs = @(
            @{ category = 'SignInLogs'; enabled = $true },
            @{ category = 'RiskyUsers'; enabled = $true },
            @{ category = 'UserRiskEvents'; enabled = $true },
            @{ category = 'NonInteractiveUserSignInLogs'; enabled = $true }
        )
    }
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 6

Invoke-AzRestMethod -Method PUT \
    -Path '/providers/Microsoft.Aadiam/diagnosticSettings/RiskToSentinel?api-version=2017-04-01-preview' \
    -Payload $diagBody

# Once streaming, create a Sentinel analytics rule:
# - Query: AADUserRiskEvents | where RiskLevel == 'high' | where RiskEventType == 'leakedCredentials'
# - Frequency: Every 5 minutes
# - Lookup: 5 minutes
# - Trigger: Results > 0
# - Severity: High
# - Automated response: Playbook to disable account + notify SOC
Managing False Positives

Sign-in risk policies set for MFA on medium risk are designed to be self-healing for legitimate users: the user completes MFA, the risk remediates, and they proceed normally. Block policies for high risk may generate FPs for users with corporate VPNs or frequent travelers. These patterns and their remediations:

# Common sign-in risk false positive: VPN with Tor-like anonymizing exit
# Solution: add VPN egress IPs to a named trusted location

# Create named location for corporate VPN
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/conditionalAccess/namedLocations' \
    -Body (@{
        '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.ipNamedLocation'
        DisplayName = 'Corporate VPN Egress'
        IsTrusted = $true
        IpRanges = @(
            @{ '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.iPv4CidrRange'; CidrAddress = '10.0.0.0/8' },
            @{ '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.iPv4CidrRange'; CidrAddress = '198.51.100.0/24' }
        )
    } | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'

# Common FP: user traveling internationally
# The 'atypical travel' detection fires when sign-ins from geographically
# distant locations happen within hours. This is common for frequent fliers.
# Solution options:
#   1. Require MFA (tier 2 policy) rather than block — user can self-remediate
#   2. Create a Trusted Travelers exclusion group; exclude from atypical-travel detection scope
#      (Not directly configurable; handled by adjusting CA policy scope)

# Dismiss atypical travel FP for a specific user via API:
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyUsers/dismiss' \
    -Body (@{ userIds = @('<user-object-id>') } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'
MFA Registration Readiness Before Enabling Risk Policies

Risk-based CA policies that require MFA or password change as grant controls only work if users have registered the required methods. Deploying these policies before registration is complete causes legitimate users to be locked out — they match the risk condition but cannot satisfy the grant control.
Scenario
Result without MFA Registered
Fix
Medium risk → MFA required by CA
User blocked — cannot satisfy MFA grant (no methods registered)
Pre-register users with MFA before enabling risk-based CA policies
High risk → Block
User blocked regardless of MFA status
Intended behavior — investigate the sign-in
TAP (one-time) used on medium-risk sign-in
TAP satisfies MFA for that session; risk auto-remediates
User should then register a permanent MFA method (Authenticator, FIDO2)
SSPR not registered for user-risk policy
User cannot complete password change grant control — locked out
Ensure SSPR is enabled and registered before deploying user-risk CA policies
Run an MFA registration campaign (SSPR + MFA combined registration) before enabling any risk-based CA policies.
Check registration completeness: Get-MgReportAuthenticationMethodUserRegistrationDetail | Where-Object { -not $_.IsMfaRegistered }
Use Temporary Access Pass (TAP) to unblock users who are locked out and need to register MFA methods.
Target risk policies at a pilot group first; validate that all pilot users have registered MFA; then expand.
Summary
Sign-in risk evaluates each authentication event for suspicious signals; risk levels (High, Medium, Low) drive CA policy enforcement.
Real-time detections can block sign-ins before token issuance; offline detections update risk levels hours later.
Deploy a three-tier CA policy set: block High, require MFA for Medium, allow Low — in Report-Only mode first.
When a user satisfies an MFA grant control on a risky sign-in, Identity Protection auto-remediates the sign-in risk event.
AiTM phishing is the most severe sign-in risk detection; standard MFA cannot prevent it — only FIDO2 and WHfB (phishing-resistant methods) do.
Admin actions (confirm compromised, dismiss) operate at the user level, affecting all associated sign-in risk events for that user.
